questions.stem: A country has an absolute advantage in producing a good when it can do what?
options: Sell the good only in its home market
Produce the good without using any resources
Produce more of the good from the same resources than another country
Produce the good at a higher opportunity cost than another country
questions.explanation: Absolute advantage means a country can produce more of a good with a given quantity of resources, or the same output with fewer resources, than another country. It is about productivity, not opportunity cost.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The theory of comparative advantage says a country should specialise in goods for which it has what?
options: The largest population
The highest tariffs
The highest opportunity cost
The lowest opportunity cost
questions.explanation: Comparative advantage holds that a country gains by specialising in goods it can produce at a lower opportunity cost than others, and trading for the rest. This raises total output even if one country is better at everything.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Comparative advantage shows that mutually beneficial trade is possible even when one country has an absolute advantage in what?
options: Both goods being produced
Neither good
Only raw materials
Only services
questions.explanation: Even if one country is more productive in both goods, it cannot have a comparative advantage in both. Each country has a lower opportunity cost in something, so specialisation and trade still benefit both.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If a country can produce either 10 units of cloth or 5 units of wine with its resources, the opportunity cost of 1 unit of wine is:
options: 2 units of cloth
0.5 units of cloth
10 units of cloth
5 units of cloth
questions.explanation: Giving up all wine production frees enough resources for 10 cloth, so 5 wine costs 10 cloth. Dividing, 1 wine costs $10 / 5 = 2$ units of cloth.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The main gain that international specialisation and trade offer is what?
options: Guaranteed equal incomes across countries
The elimination of all competition
A higher total output and consumption than without trade
Complete self sufficiency for every country
questions.explanation: By specialising according to comparative advantage and trading, countries can together produce and consume more than if each tried to make everything itself. Greater world output is the central gain from trade.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The 'terms of trade' measure what?
options: The level of tariffs on imports
The ratio of export prices to import prices
The size of the trade deficit
The total volume of goods traded
questions.explanation: The terms of trade are an index of average export prices relative to average import prices. An improvement means each unit of exports buys more imports than before.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A tariff is best described as what?
options: A tax on imported goods
A payment to domestic exporters
A limit on the quantity of imports
A ban on all foreign trade
questions.explanation: A tariff is a tax levied on imports, which raises their price in the home market. A quantitative limit is a quota, a payment to producers is a subsidy, and a total ban is an embargo.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A quota is a form of trade protection that does what?
options: Taxes exports leaving the country
Subsidises foreign producers
Fixes the exchange rate
Sets a physical limit on the quantity of a good that may be imported
questions.explanation: A quota directly restricts the quantity of a good that can be imported during a period. By limiting supply, it tends to raise the domestic price and protect home producers.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following is a common argument used to justify protectionism?
options: To protect new 'infant' industries until they can compete
To reduce domestic employment
To raise the opportunity cost of all goods
To guarantee a permanent trade deficit
questions.explanation: The infant industry argument says new industries need temporary protection to grow until they reach a competitive scale. Other arguments include protecting jobs and guarding against dumping.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A common economic argument AGAINST tariffs and quotas is that they do what?
options: Remove the need for any domestic production
Always increase total world output
Raise prices for consumers and reduce overall efficiency
Lower prices for domestic consumers
questions.explanation: Protection shields less efficient domestic producers, raises prices for consumers, and reduces the gains from specialisation. It can also invite retaliation, lowering world trade and welfare.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The balance of payments is a record of what?
options: All economic transactions between a country and the rest of the world
Only domestic retail sales
Only the money supply
Only the government's budget
questions.explanation: The balance of payments records all transactions between residents of a country and the rest of the world over a period, including trade in goods and services and flows of income and capital.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The current account of the balance of payments includes which of the following?
options: Only long term loans from abroad
Only changes in the central bank's gold reserves
Trade in goods and services, plus income and transfers
Only purchases of foreign factories
questions.explanation: The current account covers exports and imports of goods and services, net income from abroad, and net current transfers. Flows of capital and investment are recorded in the financial (capital) account.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A country has a current account deficit when what is true?
options: Its exports exceed its imports
The value of its imports of goods and services exceeds its exports plus net income
Its money supply is falling
Its government budget is balanced
questions.explanation: A current account deficit means more is paid out for imports, income, and transfers than is received from exports and income. The country is, in effect, spending more abroad than it earns.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The 'balance of trade' refers specifically to what?
options: The level of foreign exchange reserves
The total of all government spending
The difference between exports and imports of goods
The difference between saving and investment
questions.explanation: The balance of trade, sometimes called the visible balance when limited to goods, is exports minus imports. It is one part of the wider current account.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If the demand for a country's currency rises on the foreign exchange market under a floating system, the currency will tend to do what?
options: Lose its legal tender status
Stay exactly fixed
Depreciate (fall in value)
Appreciate (rise in value)
questions.explanation: Under a floating exchange rate, the price of a currency is set by supply and demand. Greater demand for the currency raises its value against others, that is the currency appreciates.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A depreciation of the domestic currency makes a country's exports and imports change in which way?
options: Both exports and imports dearer
Both exports and imports cheaper
Exports dearer abroad and imports cheaper at home
Exports cheaper abroad and imports dearer at home
questions.explanation: When the currency falls in value, foreigners need less of their money to buy the same exports, so exports become cheaper abroad, while imports cost more in domestic currency. This tends to improve the trade balance.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Under a freely floating exchange rate system, the value of a currency is determined by what?
options: A rate fixed by the central bank and never changed
The level of domestic income tax
The size of the population
Supply of and demand for the currency in the foreign exchange market
questions.explanation: A floating rate is set by market forces: the interaction of supply and demand for the currency. There is no official target rate, so the rate moves continuously with market conditions.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Under a fixed exchange rate system, how does the central bank keep the rate at its target when there is downward pressure on the currency?
options: By banning all imports
By printing more of its own currency to sell
By using its reserves to buy its own currency
By raising income tax
questions.explanation: To defend a fixed rate against depreciation, the central bank buys its own currency using foreign exchange reserves, raising demand for it. It may also raise interest rates to attract inflows.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: An 'embargo' on trade with a particular country is what?
options: A subsidy to exporters in that country
A complete ban on trade with that country
A fixed exchange rate with that country
A small tax on imports from that country
questions.explanation: An embargo is an outright prohibition on trade, often for political reasons. It is the most extreme form of trade restriction, going beyond tariffs or quotas.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: An export subsidy given to domestic firms tends to do what?
options: Raise the price of exports for foreign buyers
Reduce domestic production of the good
Lower the price of exports and make them more competitive abroad
Act exactly like an import tariff
questions.explanation: A subsidy lowers producers' costs, allowing them to sell exports more cheaply on world markets and gain competitiveness. Critics argue this distorts trade and can provoke disputes.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: 'Dumping' in international trade refers to what?
options: Importing goods at very high prices
Refusing to trade with neighbours
Selling exports abroad below cost or below the home market price
Raising tariffs on all goods
questions.explanation: Dumping is selling goods in a foreign market at a price below cost or below the price charged at home, often to gain market share. Anti-dumping duties are a common policy response.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A free trade area is best described as an arrangement where member countries do what?
options: Ban trade with each other
Adopt a single shared currency immediately
Remove tariffs and quotas on trade among themselves
Fix all their exchange rates to gold
questions.explanation: In a free trade area, members abolish tariffs and quotas on trade between themselves while each keeps its own trade policy towards outside countries. A customs union goes further with a common external tariff.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following would be recorded as a credit (inflow) on the current account?
options: Interest paid to foreign lenders
Revenue earned from exporting cocoa
A domestic resident's holiday abroad
Spending on imported machinery
questions.explanation: Exports bring money into the country, so export revenue is a credit on the current account. Imports, spending abroad, and income paid out are debits that take money out.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A persistent current account deficit may be a concern because it often must be financed by what?
options: Raising the birth rate
Borrowing from abroad or running down foreign reserves
Increasing the domestic money supply only
Cutting all government spending to zero
questions.explanation: A current account deficit means a country is buying more from abroad than it earns, so the gap must be covered by capital inflows, foreign borrowing, or drawing down reserves. Sustained deficits can build up external debt.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Free trade tends to benefit consumers mainly because it does what?
options: Widens choice and lowers prices through competition and specialisation
Eliminates the need for any imports
Guarantees every domestic firm stays in business
Raises tariffs on competing goods
questions.explanation: By opening markets to competition and allowing specialisation, free trade gives consumers access to cheaper and more varied goods. The main losers can be uncompetitive domestic producers.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics
